channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ICE Killing in Houston Puts Focus on Surge in Immigration Arrests
item.description: The shooting death of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo occurred as immigration enforcement has ramped up across the country, with thousands being arrested daily.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa, Hamed Aleaziz and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Ronaldo Schemidt/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Representative Sylvia Garcia spoke during a news conference on the case of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston on Friday.

item.title: Houston to Investigate How ICE Agent Came to Kill a Mexican Immigrant
item.description: The agency said Lorenzo Salgado Araujo tried to ram agents with a van before one shot him dead. A lawyer for his passengers said that was untrue.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: The site where a federal immigration officer shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston.

item.title: ICE Deports Immigrant Who Was Pardoned for Sex Abuse Conviction
item.description: Minnesota state officials had granted a pardon to Tou Lue Vang, a Hmong man, drawing criticism that Democratic leaders were thwarting efforts to expel criminals.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Tim Walz of Minnesota granted a pardon to Tou Lue Vang.

item.title: Trump Cuts Habitat Protections for Endangered Species
item.description: The rule change ends a safeguard that had been in place for 50 years and could hasten the demise of imperiled animals.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Piping plovers need undisturbed beaches to nest and raise their young.

item.title: Trump Administration Taps Climate Science Critic to Oversee National Climate Assessment
item.description: Matthew M. Wielicki has criticized the scientific consensus on global warming as alarmist. Now he’ll oversee a major federal report on the topic.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Go Nakamura for The New York Times
item.media:description: A scorched road in Hawaii in 2023 after devastating wildfires. The last climate assessment, published that year, found that human-caused global warming was supercharging wildfires in the West, droughts in the Great Plains and heat waves across the country.

item.title: He Wanted to Track Microplastics in the Sea. The E.P.A. Fired Him.
item.description: The official urged requiring a fish farm to monitor for the tiny plastic particles. The E.P.A. said he had taken steps to embarrass the agency.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi

item.title: Trump Says Negotiations With Iran Will Continue as U.S. Ramps Up Strikes
item.description: U.S. forces hit more than 170 targets, including air defense systems, drone and missile storage sites, and military speedboats.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: The funeral procession in Tehran on Thursday for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran’s former leader, who was killed in February during U.S. and Israeli military strikes on his country.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Will Soon Be Declared Open to All Traffic, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: The officials said that Iranian negotiators blamed the recent ship attacks on rogue military units.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels anchored off the coast of Oman, just outside the Strait of Hormuz, last month.

item.title: Shippers Face Deepening Dilemma as U.S. and Iran Vie for Control of the Strait
item.description: Companies desperately want to get their ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz, but face mounting risks. “Things are becoming uglier by the minute,” one executive said.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Jenny Gross and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Ships anchored off Oman’s coast near the Strait of Hormuz. Ship operators in the area are eager to resume normal business but face great peril if they do.

item.title: Trump Is Promoting ‘Freedom Fuel’ for $3.47 a Gallon. Who’s Behind It?
item.description: The president has praised a network of 25 recently rebranded gas stations in the Philadelphia region that have been selling gas for considerably less than the national average.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch and Mark Bonamo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: A former Valero station is one in a chain of 25 newly branded Freedom Fuel gas stations in the Philadelphia region, promoted by President Trump and the White House.

item.title: Witnesses to ICE Killing Dispute the Official Account
item.description: Also, a major housing bill could become law without Trump’s signature. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: The site where a federal immigration officer shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston.

item.title: Democrats Haunted by Biden-Harris Switch as They Replace Platner in Maine
item.description: The last-minute scramble to name a replacement reminds some in the party of the challenges they faced in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times

item.title: Graham Platner Files Paperwork to Withdraw From Maine Senate Race
item.description: The move paved the way for Democrats to choose a new nominee to challenge Senator Susan Collins in a race both parties see as key to the control of the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner during his primary watch party last month.

item.title: Maine Democrats Eyeing Late July Convention to Pick Platner Replacement
item.description: With plans still being finalized, officials are discussing a convention some time during the weekend of July 25-26 and are weighing options for choosing delegates.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Augusta, the state’s capital, is, along with Bangor, one of the possible locations of the Maine Democratic Party’s convention in late July.

item.title: If Florida Slashes Property Taxes, Where Will the Money Come From?
item.description: Gov. Ron DeSantis pushed for a proposal to slash property taxes. But after some local Republican officials protested, he backpedaled, sort of.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Zack Wittman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The population has boomed in places like Land O’ Lakes in Pasco County, Fla.

item.title: Trump Says He Won’t Sign Housing Bill, in Protest Over Stalled Voting Measure
item.description: The president did not say he would veto the housing legislation, so it would still become law unless he does. But his pronouncement reflects a growing rift between him and Senate Republicans.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Senator John Thune, the majority leader, at the Capitol last month, on the day he had been set to sign the bipartisan housing bill.

item.title: An Aging Assassin Was Caught by ICE. Will the U.S. Deport Him?
item.description: He helped plot a car bombing in Washington that killed two people in 1976. Fifty years later, old government secrets could decide his fate.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Carlos Fernando Mendez/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: Armando Fernandez Larios in 2003. For years, the U.S. government regarded Mr. Fernandez as a helpful Cold War ally of sorts, even as its relationship with Chile deteriorated.

item.title: Flooding in Missouri Prompts State of Emergency and at Least 100 Rescues
item.description: Gov. Mike Kehoe declared a state of emergency after up to a foot of rain fell overnight in the southeastern part of the state. More storms are in the forecast.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: How a Deadly Storm Surprised Wisconsin Boaters
item.description: Three children died last weekend when a storm whipped up 80 mile-per-hour winds and large waves on Geneva Lake in Wisconsin.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: WISN, via Associated Press

item.title: The Work of Helping A.I. Destroy Work
item.description: Start-ups are paying white-collar professionals to teach their jobs to artificial intelligence models. It’s a bonanza. It’s bleak. Where will it end?
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: This Star Just Ate a Planet, and It’s Not Done Yet
item.description: A star 1,300 light-years away appears to have just consumed one world and is gearing up for seconds.
item.dc:creator: Becky Ferreira

item.title: What China’s Successful Rocket Launch Means for the Future of the Space Race
item.description: A space neophyte not long ago, China is now the United State’s main competitor for supremacy throughout the solar system.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Xing Guangli/Xinhua, via Associated Press
item.media:description: An image released by the Chinese state-run Xinhua News Agency purporting to show the returning first stage of the Long March 10B rocket on a seaborne platform in the South China Sea on Friday.

item.title: F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry
item.description: A start-up company has permission to try its plan to bounce solar rays onto the dark side of Earth, turning night to day for a three-mile-wide patch.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Reflect Orbital
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of space mirrors in orbit.

item.title: This Is a Lot More Worrying Than the Supreme Court’s Ruling on Executive Power
item.description: The White House is sidestepping Congress to make huge decisions for even the most important private industries.
item.dc:creator: David Lawrence

item.title: Does Matt Damon Look Greek? No. So Stop Complaining About Lupita Nyong’o.
item.description: Helen of Troy was a legendary beauty, but also so much more.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Mendelsohn
item.media:credit: Illustration by Sam Whitney/The New York Times

item.title: New York Times Accuses Federal Employment Agency of Retaliation
item.description: A May lawsuit by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission claims the newspaper discriminated against a white male employee who did not get a promotion.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times filed a counterclaim against the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission in federal court on Friday.
